result:
A pocket-sized wish with planet-sized impact: embed empathy at hardware-level in every human brain—so the moment anyone imagines hurting another, they also have to feel what their victim would feel, vividly, unavoidably, for ten full seconds.

Nothing else changes overnight: intelligence, ambition, politics, hunger, money, beliefs, technology, or national borders. But the cost-benefit calculus of cruelty flips. War becomes psychosomatic civil war; a soldier’s bayonet stabs the soldier first. Corporate polluters gag on asthma they’re about to gift to a town downstream. A derogatory tweet literally bruises the author before it bruises the timeline. Over a single generation children grow up in a world where cruelty is retroactively self-inflicted, cooperation is self-interest spelled differently, and empathy is no longer a teachable “soft skill”—it’s the default operating system.

The paradox is lovely: humanity’s hardest problem was never physics or epidemiology; it’s the impedance mismatch between my sensory cortex and your pain. Remove that mismatch and resource allocation, justice, even climate, become technical details we *solve* instead of weaponize.

Consequences? Some industries vanish (private prisons, arms fairs, rage-farming news channels). Some dark art forms mutate, but many flourish—horror stories still scratch an itch when everyone knows nobody was truly harmed. And reciprocity becomes so productive that by century’s end we’re more astonished the former species ever scheduled genocide on its calendar than we are at fusion breakthroughs or off-world settlements.

One neural line of code. One generation. A chain reaction of mirror neurons turned up to eleven. After that, the rest of history feels like gardening.

prompt: If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
